coredata.dc:description:
Background The current synthetic strategies for carbon dots (CDs) are usually time-consuming, rely on complicated processes, and need high temperatures and energy. Recent studies have successfully synthesized CDs at room temperature. Unfortunately, most CDs synthesized at room temperature are obtained under harsh reaction conditions, prepared using aromatic precursors, or need a long time to generate. Therefore, an energy-free room-temperature rapid synthesis of CDs under mild conditions using aliphatic substrates is important. We aim to provide an innovative approach to synthesizing CDs to be used to develop the first fluorescence-based assay of the non-fluorescent anti-hyperlipidemic drug, fenofibrate.
Results We report an innovative, energy-free, and room-temperature preparation of fluorescent N-doped CDs utilizing aliphatic substrates in only 20 min. The synthesis was based on a self-exothermic Schiff base condensation reaction between methylglyoxal and ethylenediamine. The prepared CDs' antibacterial properties, biocompatibility, and cell-imaging ability were investigated. The fluorescence signal of the CDs was quantitively quenched upon adding increasing concentrations of fenofibrate in the range of 0.50–15.0 μg/mL. Therefore, the prepared CDs were applied as a nanosensor to develop the first fluorescence-based assay of fenofibrate. The reliability of the synthesized nanosensor was confirmed by the successful quantification of fenofibrate in pharmaceutical dosage forms, environmental water, weight loss herbal products, and dietary supplements. The obtained recovery ranged from 95.33 to 104.58 %. In addition, the minimal environmental impact of the developed fenofibrate sensing strategy was confirmed using the recently reported metrics.
Significance and novelty The key advantage of this work is the use of an energy-free approach to synthesize CDs rapidly under mild conditions without aromatic substrates. This opens a new window for the eco-friendly synthesis of CDs that avoids the drawbacks of the traditional methods. Additionally, it is the first fluorescence nanosensor for sensing fenofibrate in various matrices, avoiding the limitations of the previous methods, such as high cost, poor selectivity, and low sensitivity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current synthetic strategies for carbon dots (CDs) are usually time-consuming, rely on complicated processes, and need high temperatures and energy. Recent studies have successfully synthesized CDs at room temperature. Unfortunately, most CDs synthesized at room temperature are obtained under harsh reaction conditions, prepared using aromatic precursors, or need a long time to generate. Therefore, an energy-free room-temperature rapid synthesis of CDs under mild conditions using aliphatic substrates is important. We aim to provide an innovative approach to synthesizing CDs to be used to develop the first fluorescence-based assay of the non-fluorescent anti-hyperlipidemic drug, fenofibrate.

We report an innovative, energy-free, and room-temperature preparation of fluorescent N-doped CDs utilizing aliphatic substrates in only 20 min. The synthesis was based on a self-exothermic Schiff base condensation reaction between methylglyoxal and ethylenediamine. The prepared CDs' antibacterial properties, biocompatibility, and cell-imaging ability were investigated. The fluorescence signal of the CDs was quantitively quenched upon adding increasing concentrations of fenofibrate in the range of 0.50–15.0 μg/mL. Therefore, the prepared CDs were applied as a nanosensor to develop the first fluorescence-based assay of fenofibrate. The reliability of the synthesized nanosensor was confirmed by the successful quantification of fenofibrate in pharmaceutical dosage forms, environmental water, weight loss herbal products, and dietary supplements. The obtained recovery ranged from 95.33 to 104.58 %. In addition, the minimal environmental impact of the developed fenofibrate sensing strategy was confirmed using the recently reported metrics.

The key advantage of this work is the use of an energy-free approach to synthesize CDs rapidly under mild conditions without aromatic substrates. This opens a new window for the eco-friendly synthesis of CDs that avoids the drawbacks of the traditional methods. Additionally, it is the first fluorescence nanosensor for sensing fenofibrate in various matrices, avoiding the limitations of the previous methods, such as high cost, poor selectivity, and low sensitivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials have versatile applications in analytical chemistry [1–3]. Carbon dots (CDs), a category of carbon nanomaterials, have gained great interest in the past few years because of their fascinating features. These features include non-toxicity, water solubility, biocompatibility, and tunable luminescent properties [4,5]. Different approaches for synthesizing CDs exist, such as microwave, hydrothermal, and solvothermal methods. Despite their efficiency, these approaches have drawbacks, such as operating at high temperatures or time consumption [6]. Hence, room temperature synthesis as an energy-free approach for the green synthesis of CDs has recently drawn great attention [7,8]. The key advantage of this approach is avoiding energy-consuming steps associated with traditional synthesis methods. However, the currently reported room temperature synthesis methods require the induction of oxidation or polymerization of carbon precursors. This could be achieved by using high concentrations of alkali [9], acid [10], or oxidizing agents [11] in the reaction system. In addition, these methods still have some limitations, such as unsafe procedures [12] and/or long synthesis time [13]. Thus, creating straightforward and environmentally friendly processes for manufacturing CDs at room temperature without harsh conditions is crucial.

Methylglyoxal (MGO), a natural α-dicarbonyl compound, is endogenously formed in humans and plants and can also be generated during food processing [14]. Owing to its high reactivity, we used MGO as a substrate to produce CDs for the first time. The principle of the synthesis is based on the reaction between aldehyde and amine to produce Schiff base at room temperature. Herein, MGO serves as a C source, while ethylenediamine (EDA) is used as a C and N source. Simple mixing of MGO and EDA at room temperature spontaneously releases a lot of heat. This heat leads to carbonization, aromatization, polymerization, and in-situ formation of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) in about 20 min only. The described one-pot self-heating strategy offers a facile, cost-effective, sustainable, and fast approach to synthesizing CDs without needing any external energy source. In addition, it relies only on aliphatic precursors and avoids aromatic ones. These features make the described approach well-matched with the principles of green chemistry.

Fenofibrate (FEN) is an orally administered lipid modulator. Its action is to lower low-density lipoprotein (LDL), triglycerides, and total cholesterol and raise high-density lipoprotein (HDL) [15]. FEN belongs to a class of drugs called fibrates, which are characterized by their high persistence and tendency to bioaccumulate. Fibrates are consistently discharged into aquatic environments due to their extensive consumption and persistent nature. This discharge may have negative impacts on both humans and aquatic species [16]. In addition, fibrates are among the synthetic drugs that are illegally added to weight loss dietary supplements and herbal products. The manufacturers illegally add these drugs to improve their products’ efficiency. Excess and prolonged consumption of adulterated herbal products and dietary supplements increases the possibility of developing serious toxicity and adverse effects [17]. Therefore, a reliable, facile, and sensitive approach to quantify FEN is of great value.

Different analytical methodologies were applied to determine FEN, such as HPLC [18–24], HPTLC [25–27], spectrophotometry [18,19,22,28–30], and voltammetry [31,32]. However, there are several drawbacks to these methods. HPLC methods [18–24] are time- and energy-consuming, costly, and utilize toxic solvents. HPTLC methods [25–27] use highly toxic organic solvents such as toluene, chloroform, and hexane. Spectrophotometric methods [18,19,22,28–30] suffer from poor selectivity and sensitivity, which are essential for analyzing environmental and dietary matrices. The reported voltammetric methods [31,32] rely on mercury electrodes, a highly toxic heavy metal. In contrast, fluorescence spectroscopy is a simple, highly sensitive, and eco-friendly analytical technique [33,34]. To date, no spectrofluorimetric assay for FEN has been reported. This motivated us to develop the first spectrofluorimetric method for its analysis.

This work proposed an innovative strategy for preparing fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs using MGO and EDA for the first time. The synthesis was entirely conducted at ambient temperature without an external energy source. FEN quenches the fluorescence of the as-prepared N-CDs quantitatively. Hence, the N-CDs could be utilized as a fluorescent nanosensor for the first fluorimetric determination of FEN. The established method has several practical applications, such as determining FEN in pharmaceutical, environmental, and herbal matrices. In addition, four recent metrics confirmed the eco-friendliness of the proposed approach.

The used reagents, chemicals, and instruments, as well as the procedure for determining quantum yield, antibacterial activity, biocompatibility, and cellular imaging of N-CDs, are described in the supplementary file.

MGO and EDA were used to synthesize N-CDs at room temperature. For this, 0.9 mL of EDA (equivalent to 0.9 g) was mixed with 1.8 mL of 40 % w/v MGO (equivalent to 0.87 g) in a 15-mL round glass bottle. After 20 min, the mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 3000 rpm. The supernatant was filtered using a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm and kept refrigerated at 4 °C in a dark bottle. To calculate the production yield, the N-CDs were freeze-dried. The obtained powder was about 1.64 g, while the theoretical yield was 1.77 g (0.9 + 0.87). Hence, our room temperature synthesis approach of N-CDs has a high production yield (92.6 %). To prepare the working solution of N-CDs, 25 μL of the as-prepared N-CDs (equivalent to 15.2 mg) was placed in a 50-mL volumetric flask; then deionized water was added to complete the volume to the mark.

Aliquots of 250 μL of the working solution of N-CDs were placed in 10-mL calibrated flasks. Then, mixed with appropriate volumes from the FEN working standard solution to get a final concentration range of 0.50–15.0 μg/mL. Deionized water was added to complete the volume to 10 mL. The fluorescence quenching (F0/F) was recorded at λex/λem of 300/410 nm, where F0 and F mean the fluorescence of the N-CDs alone and with FEN, respectively. F0/F was plotted versus FEN concentration (μg/mL) to construct the calibration plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of FEN in tablets and capsules
ce:section.ce:para: Ten tablets of Lipanthyl Supra® (160 mg FEN/tablet) were weighed, triturated, and mixed well. The contents of ten Lipanthyl® capsules (300 mg FEN/capsule) were emptied, accurately weighted, and mixed well. The amounts required to obtain a final concentration of 0.1 mg/mL of FEN were accurately weighed and carefully placed in 50-mL calibrated flasks. Then mixed with 30 mL methanol. After sonication for 30 min, methanol was added to complete the volume to 50 mL. The resulting mixtures were filtered using 0.22 μm membrane filters. Appropriate aliquots were placed in a group of 10-mL calibrated flasks and completed as mentioned in Section 2.2.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of FEN in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples were filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filters before analysis. Into 10-mL calibrated flasks, 1.0 ml of water sample was mixed with 250 μL of N-CDs working solution. Then, deionized water was added to complete the volume to 10 mL. Spiked samples were prepared by transferring appropriate aliquots of FEN working solution (final concentration range is 0.50–15.0 μg/mL) to a series of 10-mL calibrated flasks. To each flask, 250 μL of N-CDs working solution and 1.0 mL of water sample were added. Deionized water was added to complete the volume to 10 mL. All water samples were assayed as mentioned in Section 2.2.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of FEN as an adulterant in dietary supplements and herbal products
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed strategy was utilized to determine FEN as an adulterant in dietary supplements and herbal products used for weight loss. Two commercial products were tested: Chito 500® capsules, each capsule containing 500 mg of chitosan, and Slim Trim® capsules, each capsule containing green tea powder extract (200 mg), Garcia Cambogia powder extract (150 mg), Ginger powder extract (50 mg), and chromium picolinate (10 μg).

Sample preparation was conducted employing ultrasonication and centrifugation. In a typical procedure, the content of ten capsules of the investigated real samples was combined. An amount of 10 mg from each sample was extracted ultrasonically using 50 mL of acetonitrile for 30 min. Then, it was centrifugated for 5 min at 5000 rpm and filtered using 0.22 μm membrane filters before analysis. An aliquot of 250 μL of the clear filtrate was placed in a 10-mL calibrated flask containing 250 μL of N-CDs and then assayed as mentioned in Section 2.2.1. Spiked samples were obtained by pre-mixing the pure powder of FEN with the solid content of the investigated sample. The same procedure was followed to extract FEN, which was then analyzed in the same way as non-spiked samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesizing CDs at room temperature is challenging since the production of CDs requires a high temperature for a carbonization step to occur [35]. A smart solution for this problem is to use starting materials that give off a lot of heat when they meet at room temperature [36]. We used MGO and EDA to synthesize N-CDs at room temperature, considering their exothermic reaction. Upon adding EDA to MGO, the yellow color of MGO is rapidly converted to dark brown. This is accompanied by a rapid temperature rise from 21 °C to 74 °C for 60 s, followed by a gradual decrease during the next 20 min (Fig. 1 a), indicating a self-exothermic reaction between MGO and EDA. The self-exothermic reaction involves Schiff base formation (CN bond) that resulted from a condensation reaction between MGO's carbonyl group (CO) and EDA's primary amine group (NH2). The impact of the ratio of MGO and EDA on the quantum yield of the N-CDs was studied to optimize the synthesis process. The highest quantum yield (10.02 %) was achieved using MGO and EDA in the ratio of (2: 1), which was selected as the optimum synthesis condition (Fig. 1b).

1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum of resulted N-CDs (Fig. 1c) shows signals at 1–4 and 7–8.5 ppm corresponding to aliphatic and aromatic protons, respectively [37]. The presence of aromatic protons confirms the aromatization and successful generation of CDs from the used aliphatic substrates. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image confirmed the formation of N-CDs (Fig. 1d). The obtained N-CDs were uniformly distributed spherical particles without aggregation. They had a small diameter range from 2 to 5 nm (Fig. 1c) and an average diameter of 3.34 nm. A high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1e, revealed the absence of crystal lattice in the N-CDs, indicating their amorphous nature [13]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) further confirmed the amorphous nature of the N-CDs, as revealed by the diffused ring pattern shown in Fig. 1f [38]. Moreover, the broad X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the N-CDs shown in Fig. 2 a further indicates their amorphous nature [38,39].

The N-CDs' surface functional groups were examined through Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2b. The wide peak at 3300 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H. The small band at 2100 cm−1 is assigned to C C stretching vibration. The narrow peak at 1630 cm−1 corresponds to CN/CO/CC stretching vibrations. The peak at 1050 cm−1 corresponds to C–N stretching. The peak at 610 cm−1 is indicative of C–H bending [40]. The composition of the produced N-CDs was further investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS full scan shown in Fig. 2c confirms that the N-CDs consist of C (74.33 %), N (14.9 %), and O (10.77 %), as revealed from their dominant peaks at 285.49, 399.85, and 531.66 eV, respectively [39]. The C1s spectrum shown (Fig. 2d) indicates the presence of different types of C: CC/C–C, C–N, and C–O at 284.32, 285.48, and 286.68 eV, respectively [41]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows peaks at 398.57 and 399.72 eV, representing pyridinic N and amine N, respectively [42]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum is mainly composed of two peaks at 530.38 eV (C–O) and 531.27 eV (CO), as presented in Fig. 2f [43]. Furthermore, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum (Fig. S1) and elemental mapping (Fig. S2) of the as-prepared CDs prove that they consist of C, N, and O.

The UV–visible spectrum of the N-CDs (Fig. 3 a) shows strong absorption centered at 290 nm in the UV range, with a tail continuing to the visible range. The spectrum resembles the typical UV–visible spectrum of CDs [44]. The N-CDs exhibit λex/λem of 318/410 nm, as displayed in Fig. 3a. The as-prepared N-CDs show excitation-dependent emission behavior over the range of 280–380 nm (Fig. 3b), probably resulting from their particle size distribution [45]. Excitation-dependent emission can also be shown by examining the fluorescence contour map (Fig. 3c), which reveals changes in maximum emission wavelength upon changing the excitation wavelength.

Since the stability of CDs is crucial, different experiments were conducted to investigate their stability. The fluorescence signal of the N-CDs was examined under the effect of pH in the range (2-12) as presented in Fig. 3d. The emitted fluorescence by the prepared N-CDs was remarkedly higher in acidic than alkaline pH. The highest intensity was observed in the pH range of 2–4, then gradually decreased until pH 7. In alkaline pH, the intensity increased again but slightly until pH 9 and then gradually decreased. The high signals in acidic pH could be explained by the presence of OH− groups on the surface of the N-CDs that form hydrogen bonds with H+, making the N-CDs more stable [45]. In addition, the fluorescence signal of the N-CDs showed no apparent decrease when the concentration of NaCl varied from 0 to 1 mol/L (Fig. 3e), demonstrating their excellent stability. In addition, the Zeta potential of the N-CDs was found to be −25.6 ± 1.7 mV (Fig. S3). The high negative Zeta potential increases the repulsion between the negatively charged surface groups and prevents the aggregation of the N-CDs, contributing to enhancing their stability [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs’ antibacterial activity, cytotoxicity, and cellular imaging ability
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of the N-CDs was explored using the agar disc-diffusion method against Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli and P. aeruginosa). The N-CDs exhibited antibacterial activity against the investigated bacteria, as shown in Fig. 4 . The antibacterial activity of the prepared N-CDs was compared with previously reported CDs with antibacterial activity, as displayed in Table 1 . The results indicate the opportunity to employ the N-CDs in different fields, particularly biomedical applications. As indicated above, the N-CDs are negatively charged. This excludes the electrostatic interaction between the N-CDs and bacterial cells from being the main antibacterial mechanism of the N-CDs. The antibacterial ability of the N-CDs is expected to be generated from the biologically active azomethine group (-CN) that resulted from the Schiff base reaction between MGO and EDA [47]. In addition, the nitrogen-doped CDs can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), as reported by Travlou et al. [46]. The characterization tests implemented in this study showed the richness of the prepared CDs with nitrogen, making the ROS generation another possible antibacterial mechanism.

MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay was used to examine the cytotoxicity of the N-CDs. The N-CDs revealed good biocompatibility against human hepatocellular carcinoma (HepG2) cells, as indicated by high cell viability (higher than 60 %) in the presence of high concentrations of N-CDs (Fig. S4). Therefore, bioimaging experiments were conducted. The bright-field image of HepG2 cells showed no background autofluorescence (Fig. S5a). The HepG2 cells incubated with 1 % of N-CDs emitted a blue fluorescence after excitation at 360 nm, as shown in Fig. S5b. This opens a new window for their advanced bio-imaging applications, which will be investigated in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs as a FEN fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: After adding increasing concentrations of FEN (0.50–15.0 μg/mL), the fluorescence of N-CDs gradually decreased, as presented in Fig. 5 a. In general, the fluorescence of CDs could be quenched via one or more of different pathways, such as static or dynamic quenching or inner filter effect (IFE) [52]. Therefore, different experiments were conducted to elucidate the quenching mechanism.

First, it is important to examine the overlap between the absorption spectrum of the quencher and the excitation and emission spectra of the fluorophore. This reveals if IFE contributes to the quenching. There are two types of IFE: primary and secondary IFE. The primary IFE occurs if the quencher's absorption spectrum and the fluorophore's excitation spectrum overlap. The secondary IFE happens when the absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps with the emission spectrum of the fluorophore [53]. In our case, the quenching originated from the primary IFE, as the UV–visible spectrum of FEN greatly overlaps with the excitation spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3a).

To support this assumption, the IFE was subtracted from the fluorescence signals of the (N-CDs + FEN) to determine the corrected fluorescence (Fcor) using Equation (1) [8]:
(1)

Where Fobs is the observed fluorescence signal, Aex and Aem refer to the absorbance of FEN at the λex (300 nm) and λem (410 nm) of N-CDs, respectively.

Next, % suppressed efficiency (%E) was determined for Fcor and Fobs according to Equation (2) [8]:
(2)

The plot of %E versus FEN concentration is shown in Fig. 5b. The results revealed that IFE is the main contributor to the observed quenching since almost a complete loss of suppression efficiency occurs after subtracting the IFE.

Next, the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence of the N-CDs + FEN system was examined. Since IFE is based on spectral overlaps between the fluorophore and the quencher, it is expected that changing the excitation wavelength will alter the quenching. As presented in Fig. 5c, the absorption spectrum of FEN resembles the excitation wavelength-dependent quenching spectrum of the N-CDs + FEN system, which is solid evidence of the IFE [54].

Then, the Stern-Volmer study was conducted at different temperatures (313, 323, and 333 K). It is implemented here to reveal if another mechanism, such as static or dynamic quenching, is involved in the quenching process besides IFE. Static mechanism occurs when the quencher forms a ground state complex with the fluorophore. Dynamic quenching results from collisions between the fluorophore and quencher [38]. The Stern-Volmer study was conducted using Equation (3) :
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
[ FEN ]
Where: KSV is the quenching constant, while [FEN] refers to the concentration of FEN. Increasing the temperature increases KSV in dynamic quenching while decreasing it in static quenching. In contrast, KSV is temperature-independent when the IFE is the only cause of quenching since IFE is neither a dynamic nor static mechanism [55]. Fig. 5d shows the Stern-Volmer plots at different temperatures. The calculated KSV values at 313, 323, and 333 K were 19.89 × 103, 18.94 × 103, and 22.34 × 103 mol/L, respectively. Such temperature independence pattern of KSV values confirms that IFE is the sole contributor to the observed quenching.

The variables that alter the quenching efficiency (F0/F) were carefully investigated to attain the highest possible sensitivity and robust analysis. First, the results obtained by the excitation-dependent fluorescence quenching (Fig. 5c) were used to select the optimum excitation wavelength as it could significantly affect the fluorescence quenching. The highest quenching was obtained after excitation at 290 or 300 nm. The higher wavelength (300 nm) was chosen to provide good selectivity and avoid possible interference expected in the intended environmental applications. Next, the effect of the volume of the N-CDs was studied over a range of 100–350 μL (Fig. 6a). The highest quenching was achieved using 225–275 μL. To obtain a robust assay, 250 μL was chosen as the optimum volume. Then, the impact of pH on the fluorescence quenching was examined over a pH range of 2–12 using 0.04 M Britton-Robinson buffer. The results indicate that the observed quenching was pH-independent, as shown in Fig. 6 b. In addition, different buffers were examined, including acetate (0.1 M, pH 4), phosphate (0.1 M, pH 7), and borate (0.1 M, pH 9). Also, a higher concentration of Britton-Robinson buffer (0.1 M) was tried. However, the overall quenching was not significantly affected. This could be explained by the minor impact of pH on the lambda max of FEN. The lambda max of FEN at different pH are 292 nm (acetate buffer pH 4.0), 291.2 nm (phosphate buffer pH 7.8), 291.6 nm (0.1 M NaOH), 293.6 nm (phosphate buffer pH 5.8), and 290.2 nm in distilled water [60]. Therefore, no buffer was used during the analysis, which simplifies and speeds up the analytical procedures. Finally, the impact of incubation time was investigated. The fluorescence signals of N-CDs alone and the N-CDs/FEN system were recorded for 20 min at 2-min intervals. It was found that the quenching effect appears immediately and remains stable for at least 20 min. This eliminates the need for incubation time before measurements, which gives rapidity to the proposed method and decreases the overall analysis time.
The validity of the proposed FEN sensing strategy was investigated following the instructions of the International Council for Harmonization (ICH) [61].

Under the optimum conditions, a quantifiable reduction in the fluorescence signal of the N-CDs was observed after adding FEN. The plot of FEN concentration (μg/mL) against fluorescence quenching (F0/F) demonstrated linearity between 0.50 and 15.0 μg/mL of FEN with an excellent correlation coefficient of 0.9996 (Fig. 6c). Detection (LOD) and quantitation (LOQ) limits were estimated as LOD = 3.3(S.D of blank/slope of the regression line) and LOQ = 3xLOD [61]. The estimated LOD and LOQ of the proposed FEN sensing method were 0.13 and 0.40 μg/mL, respectively, indicating its high sensitivity. The validation data are abridged in Table S1.

Five concentration levels of FEN were assayed using the proposed FEN sensing strategy to examine its accuracy. The recovery range was 97.62–102.72 % (Table S2), proving the method's accuracy. Three concentration levels of FEN were used to examine the method's intra- and inter-day precision. The low values of relative standard deviation (% RSD) shown in Table S3 confirmed the excellent precision of the suggested approach.

The impact of deliberate changes in the experimental conditions was studied to evaluate the method's robustness. These minor changes were λex (300 ± 1 nm), λem (410 ± 1 nm), and volume of N-CDs (250 ± 1 μL). The recoveries obtained in the tested condition were around 100 % (98.14–102.29 %) with very low % RSD (Table S4), proving the excellent robustness of the method.

The method's selectivity toward FEN was investigated to assess its reliability for real sample analysis. The study involved different probable interferants usually present in environmental samples, including cations (Na+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+), anions (chloride, nitrate), and other excipients such as ascorbic acid, citric acid, sucrose, and lactose. In addition, several drugs co-administered with FEN were also included, such as atorvastatin [26], simvastatin [24], rosuvastatin [30], and ezetimibe [26]. The study was performed to examine the impact of each interferant on the fluorescence signal of the N-CDs alone and with FEN. The results presented in Fig. 6d confirm the method's selectivity toward FEN and tolerability to probable interferents. The method's good selectivity resulted from operating at λex = 300 nm, as most interfering substances absorb below 300 nm. It is expected that the proposed approach will have the potential to extend its applicability to other fibrates. This could be achieved by changing the excitation wavelength to match the maximum absorption wavelength of the target analyte.

The developed FEN sensing strategy was successfully applied to determine FEN in its pharmaceutical forms (tablets and capsules), as shown in Table S5. The method showed an excellent recovery range (98.9–101.9 %) with a small SD (>2). In addition, the method's performance was statistically compared with the reported UV spectrophotometric method [29]. The t-test and F-test indicated no significant differences between the methods.

Fibrates such as FEN are a very persistent category of lipid-lowering drugs that are commonly found in environmental water and drinking water and are toxic to aquatic life [16]. Therefore, monitoring FEN in water is essential to prevent the adverse effects of uncontrolled long-term exposure to human and aquatic life. The N-CDs were applied to quantify FEN in two types of water: environmental water (river water) and potable water (tap water). As shown in Table S6, the recovery range was 95.3–103.8 % (SD <3.5), indicating the reliability of the N-CDs for determining FEN in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of FEN as an adulterant in dietary supplements and herbal products
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of FEN in dietary supplements and herbal products for weight loss directly affects their safety and efficacy. This is due to the potential health risks associated with the unregulated long-term consumption of FEN [17]. This problem is exaggerated when the individuals using these adulterated products co-administer other drugs, which may result in drug-drug interaction. Therefore, determining FEN in over-the-counter formulations for weight loss is essential to ensure their safety. The proposed FEN sensing method was applied to determine FEN in two widely used weight loss products. The method exhibited an excellent performance without interference from the components of these products. As shown in Table 2 , FEN recovery ranged from 96.7 % to 104.6 % (SD less than 4), indicating the reliability of the method for determining FEN as an adulterant in commercial weight loss products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the proposed and reported FEN sensing methods
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the proposed FEN sensing strategy was compared with previously reported FEN analytical methods, as shown in Table 3 . The proposed method shows higher sensitivity than the UV spectrophotometric methods. It also avoids the poor selectivity of these methods, which allows for expanding its applicability to include environmental and dietary matrices. Compared to chromatographic methods, our method shows a sensitivity higher than many HPLC methods. It also avoids the drawbacks associated with HPLC analysis, such as lengthy analysis time, high cost, and energy consumption. In addition, the proposed method uses water as a green diluting solvent and avoids the highly toxic solvents used in HPTLC methods, such as hexane, tetrahydrofuran [25], toluene, chloroform [26], and dichloromethane [27]. Although the reported electrochemical methods [31,32] show higher sensitivity than our method, they use mercury-based electrodes, which is one of the most toxic metals. On the other hand, our method is based on an easily synthesized nanosensor and shows high selectivity and sensitivity that permits its applicability in various matrices.

There is a global trend toward minimizing the environmental impact of analytical procedures by reducing the consumption of harmful chemicals and reagents and conserving energy. It is important to build analytical techniques that are intrinsically environmentally benign [62]. Hence, the environmental profiles of the developed FEN sensing strategy were evaluated using different recent assessment metrics.

AGREE is a user-friendly, informative, and flexible metric for greenness assessment of analytical methods based on the twelve principles of green chemistry. The output of AGREE is an easily interpreted clock-shaped pictogram. A score ranging from zero to one and a greenness-indicating color in the center of the pictogram. The method is greener when the final score is close to one, and the color is dark green. Each evaluated criterion is given a score from zero to one, and the final score is determined as their average. In addition, the graph comprises twelve segments, each assigned a color from dark green to red. This color reflects the evaluation result of each parameter, where dark green indicates the best greenness while red means the least greenness [63]. According to the AGREE tool (Fig. 7 a), the proposed FEN sensing method achieved an overall score of 0.7 with a green color, proving its eco-friendliness. The corresponding evaluation data are shown in Table S7.

GAPI is another popular greenness assessment tool for analytical procedures. It considers all procedures, including sample preparation, instrumentation, reagents, sample collection, transportation, and storage. The output of the GAPI tool is a pictogram incorporating five pentagrams, each divided into three or four portions. The evaluation involves fifteen parameters. A color is assigned for each evaluated parameter, where green, yellow, and red colors indicate low, medium, and high environmental impacts, respectively. The analytical method is more eco-friendly if there are more green portions [64].

More recently, complexGAPI was introduced as a complementary to the well-known GAPI tool. It was specially developed to assess the pre-analysis steps. It is suitable for assessing the analytical methods involving materials or compounds synthesized before analysis, such as CDs. The output of complexGAPI is similar to GAPI in addition to a hexagonal section added to the bottom of the pictogram for greenness assessment of the pre-analysis procedure [65]. Since the proposed method utilized N-CDs synthesized before analysis, complexGAPI would be more suitable for its greenness evaluation than GAPI. Fig. 7b shows the assessment of the developed approach by the complexGAPI metric, which reveals that both the steps for the N-CDs’ preparation and the assay procedure fulfill many greenness requirements. The corresponding evaluation data are shown in Table S8.

The whiteness evaluation is based on the 12 principles of white analytical chemistry (WAC). These 12 principles are categorized into three groups, each considering four parameters. The red group (R1-R4) evaluates the analytical performance of the method. The green group (G1-G4) considers the method's environmental effects. The blue group (B1–B4) assesses the practical side of the method. The abbreviation RGB is derived from these colors: red, green, and blue. The final whiteness score ranges from zero (the worst) to 100 (the optimal). It is determined by combining the individual scores of the evaluated criteria, which also range from zero to 100 [66]. The method achieved an excellent whiteness score (96.7 %), as shown in Fig. 7c.

BAGI is a new metric introduced as complementary to the well-established tools for greenness assessment of analytical methods. It mainly focuses on the practicability (blueness) of the analytical method. BAGI considers ten main factors: analysis type, number of analytes, analytical technique used, simultaneous sample preparation, sample preparation steps, sample throughput, reagents, the requirement of preconcentration, degree of automation, and amount of sample. A score is assigned to each factor where 2.5 points represent the worst score, and 10 points is the highest score. The overall score, together with an asteroid pictogram, is generated by the software. Each parameter is given a color extending from dark blue (highest practicability) to white (lowest practicability). However, the analytical method is considered practical if the final score is greater than 60 [67]. As shown in Fig. 7d, the proposed method achieved 65.0 points, confirming its good applicability. The corresponding evaluation data are shown in Table S9.

The current study introduces an innovative method to synthesize fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs at room temperature. This method achieves the synthesis in 20 min using EDA and MGO without aromatic substrates or energy input. The non-fluorescent drug FEN quantitively quenched the N-CDs’ fluorescent signal, allowing the establishment of FEN's first fluorescence assay. The developed approach was utilized to quantify FEN in tablet and capsule dosage forms with performance comparable to that of the reported method. More importantly, the strategy was employed to quantify FEN in environmental waters and over-the-counter weight loss dietary supplements and herbal products. The method achieved excellent greenness, blueness, and whiteness profiles according to the recently introduced assessment metrics. Getting rid of energy consumption, high temperature, and aromatic precursors during the synthesis of CDs opens a new window for safe, economical, and green production of CDs.
